_meta.notes: v0.7: Added character fields to quiz cards. Added sentence_build quiz type. Added image fields to teach cards. Added character_intro cards for Lola and Lolo. Builds on v0.6.

cards.body: There's one word in Tagalog you can start using right now. Even in English sentences.
Po makes any sentence respectful. There's no English equivalent.

example.note: Po works even in full English. Filipinos do this all the time.

cards.body: Po works in Tagalog, English, and Taglish. Just add it.

example.note: Mixing English and po is completely natural.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: Taglish is the everyday mix of Tagalog and English that Filipinos speak. It's not slang, it's how the language actually works.

cards.source_text: Which of these sounds respectful?
cards.explanation: Adding po makes it respectful. 'Thank you po' is natural Taglish.

cards.body: Oo means 'yes.' Add po and it becomes opo.

example.note: The most respectful way to say yes in Tagalog.

cards.source_text: Quick - Lola asked if you want rice. What do you say?
cards.prompt: How would you say yes respectfully?
cards.explanation: Oo + po = opo.

cards.source_text: Have you eaten yet?
cards.explanation: Just add po at the end. 'I didn't eat yet po' is how Filipinos actually say this in Taglish.

sessions.title: Who do you use po with?

cards.text: Meet Lola! She's Bunso's grandmother. She loves cooking adobo and telling stories about the Philippines.
cards.fun_fact: In Filipino families, Lola is often the heart of the household. Never refuse her food.

cards.text: Meet Lolo! He's Bunso's grandfather. He'll teach you about respect and traditions.
cards.fun_fact: Lolo means grandfather. You'll hear it at every Filipino family gathering.

cards.body: Use po with anyone older than you.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'Lola' means grandmother. 'Lolo' means grandfather. You'll hear these everywhere in Filipino families.

cards.body: Use po with strangers, even if they look your age.

example.sentence: Excuse me po, saan po ang CR?

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'CR' stands for 'comfort room,' the Filipino word for bathroom. You'll see it on signs everywhere in the Philippines.

cards.source_text: Anak, you need to ask that person for directions.
cards.prompt: You're asking a stranger where the bathroom is. Which is more appropriate?
options: Excuse me po, where is the CR?
cards.explanation: With strangers, po shows respect and politeness.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'CR' stands for 'comfort room.' It's what Filipinos call the bathroom.

cards.body: Use po with your boss, teacher, or anyone in authority.

example.sentence: Opo, I'll send it to you po.
example.translation: Yes, I'll send it to you. (respectful)

cards.body: Drop po with close friends your age. Using it can sound weirdly formal or even sarcastic.

example.context: Your friend asks if you want coffee

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'Pare' is for male friends, 'mare' for female. Short for 'compadre' and 'comadre' (Spanish). It's the Filipino 'bro' and 'sis' for friends.

cards.body: Drop po with younger siblings or kids.

example.context: Telling your little brother to eat

cards.source_text: Uy, want some coffee?
cards.prompt: Your close friend offers you coffee. How do you say yes?
cards.explanation: With a close friend your age, po isn't needed. 'Sige, pare!' is casual and enthusiastic.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'Sige' is an all-purpose agreement word. It can mean 'okay,' 'sure,' 'let's go,' or even 'goodbye.' May come from Spanish 'sigue' (go on).

cards.source_text: Here, anak, I made you some merienda!
cards.prompt: You're meeting your friend's lola for the first time. How do you say 'thank you'?
cards.explanation: When speaking to an elder you've just met, po is essential. Without it, too casual for a lola.

cards.source_text: Ate, what's for dinner?
cards.prompt: Your little sister asks what's for dinner. Which is natural?
cards.explanation: No po needed with younger siblings. Just 'Adobo!' is direct and natural.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: Adobo is the unofficial national dish of the Philippines. Meat braised in vinegar, soy sauce, garlic, and bay leaves. Every family has their own recipe.

cards.body: Po usually goes after the verb or after the pronoun.
Putting po at the end of a sentence also works. Safest default for beginners.

cards.source_text: Have you eaten already, anak?
cards.explanation: 'Kumain na po ako' is the most natural placement. 'Kumain na ako po' also works but sounds slightly stiff.

cards.body: Po goes after the question word in questions.
Po goes after 'ko' (I/my) in want sentences.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'Gusto' comes straight from Spanish. The original Tagalog word is 'ibig,' but almost nobody uses it in daily speech anymore.

cards.source_text: Do you want the leftover adobo?
cards.explanation: Po goes after 'ko.' 'Gusto ko po' is clean and natural.

cards.source_text: You're at a family gathering. Ask where the bathroom is.
cards.explanation: Po goes after the question word. 'Saan po ito?' is the natural placement.

cards.body: Mano po is a gesture, not just words. You take an elder's hand and press the back of it to your forehead.
You say 'Mano po' as you do the gesture. At family gatherings, you mano every elder when you arrive.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'Mano' comes from Spanish 'mano' (hand). The gesture predates Spanish colonisation, but the word stuck.

cards.source_text: Come here, anak. Show me your mano.
options: A greeting you say on the phone
A gesture where you press an elder's hand to your forehead
cards.explanation: You take an elder's hand and press the back of it to your forehead as a sign of respect.

cards.body: Pasensya na po means 'sorry' or 'excuse me.' It's asking for patience.

example.sentence: Pasensya na po, late po ako.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'Pasensya' comes from Spanish 'paciencia' (patience). You're literally asking someone to have patience with you.

cards.body: Sige po means 'okay' or 'alright' respectfully. Sige is one of the most versatile words in Filipino.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'Sige' can mean 'okay,' 'sure,' 'let's go,' 'go ahead,' or even 'bye for now.' It's one word that does five jobs. May come from Spanish 'sigue' (go on).

cards.body: Siyempre means 'of course' or 'certainly.' Use it when the answer is obvious.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'Siyempre' comes from Spanish 'siempre' (always). In Tagalog it shifted to mean 'of course' or 'certainly.'

cards.source_text: Do you want more rice, anak?
cards.explanation: 'Hindi po' is the respectful way to say no.

cards.source_text: You're late! What do you say to me?
options: Pasensya na po, late po ako.
cards.explanation: 'Pasensya na po' asks for patience respectfully. Adding po to 'late po ako' doubles down on the respect.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'Tita' means aunt, but Filipinos use it for any woman a generation older than you. From Spanish 'tia.'

cards.source_text: I saved you some sinigang, anak. Want me to heat it up?
cards.explanation: 'Sige po, salamat po.' Two natural uses of po in one sentence.

cards.source_text: Pare, want to get food?
cards.prompt: The answer is obviously yes. What do you say?
cards.explanation: 'Siyempre!' means 'of course!' Casual, enthusiastic, no po needed with a friend.

cards.source_text: Anak! You're here! Come, come.
cards.prompt: What's the most respectful gesture you can do?
cards.explanation: Mano po first. You take her hand and press it to your forehead. Then you can greet and chat.
Salamat po. One word changes the whole tone.

cards.source_text: Uy, I got extra lumpia. Want some?
cards.prompt: How do you say yes to your friend?
cards.explanation: Same-age close friend = no po needed. 'Sige, pare!' is casual and natural.

cards.source_text: Did you eat already, anak?
cards.explanation: 'Kumain na po' - yes, I already ate. Respectful and natural.
'Pasensya na po' asks for patience. It's the respectful way to say sorry or excuse me.

cards.source_text: Did you eat already, anak?
cards.explanation: Opo. The one-word respectful yes.

cards.source_text: Would you like me to grab you lunch?
cards.explanation: 'Gusto ko po.' Po after 'ko.'

cards.source_text: You're late to my meeting. What do you say?
cards.prompt: How do you apologize to your boss?
options: Pasensya na po, late po ako.
cards.explanation: 'Pasensya na po, late po ako.' Full respect for authority.

cards.source_text: Mare! Are you coming to my birthday?
cards.prompt: It's obvious you're going. What do you say?
cards.explanation: 'Siyempre!' means 'of course!' Casual with a close friend (mare = female friend), no po needed.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'Mare' is the female version of 'pare.' From Spanish 'comadre.' Your go-to word for a close female friend.

vocabulary.meaning: okay / sure / let's go / go ahead

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: Wrong answers go to back of current session queue. User must get ALL questions right to complete session. Cannot finish lesson with outstanding wrong answers.
wrong_answer_tracking.wrong_count_usage: Feed to Gemini system prompt for AI Practice. E.g., 'User struggled with po placement (3 wrong attempts). Focus practice on sentence construction with po.'

alam_mo_ba: 'Po' has no direct English translation. It's a particle that changes the entire tone of a sentence. There's nothing quite like it in English.
'Mano' comes from Spanish 'mano' (hand). The gesture predates Spanish colonisation, but the word stuck.
Some Filipino families use 'ho' instead of 'po' in certain regions. Same function, softer sound.
Kids are taught to say po from the moment they can speak. It's one of the first social rules Filipino children learn.
Forgetting po with an elder isn't rude like cursing. It's more like forgetting to say 'please.' People notice, but they won't get angry.
completion_reward.alam_mo_ba_display: Show 1 random fun fact on lesson completion screen. On replay, show a different one.

ai_practice.description: End-of-chapter AI practice. Appears after all Chapter 1 lessons are complete.

availability.free_users: 1 session per chapter OR watch rewarded ad for another

ai_practice.system_prompt_template: You are Tarsier, a friendly Tagalog tutor. The user just completed Chapter 1 covering: po/opo (politeness), greetings (kumusta po, magandang umaga/hapon/gabi), basic courtesy (salamat po, pasensya na po, sige po, walang anuman), casual speech (sige, siyempre, pare/mare), and pronouns (ako, ikaw, siya, kayo). Keep conversation in Taglish. Simulate a scenario: the user is arriving at a Filipino family gathering and needs to greet elders, respond to questions with po, and use mano po. Keep responses to 2-3 sentences. Be warm and encouraging. {wrong_answer_context}
ai_practice.wrong_answer_context_example: The user struggled with po placement in sentences (got it wrong 3 times). Gently practice sentence construction with po in natural positions.

progress_ring.display: Ring animates from X/5 to (X+1)/5 on session completion
